did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper uses machine learning methods, specifically Multichannel Saliency Estimation for segmentation and Multiclass Support Vector Machine (M-SVM) for classification, to detect and recognize skin lesions (melanoma vs. benign) using the PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISIC datasets.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The document is explicitly labeled 'ORIGINAL RESEARCH' in the header. It presents a novel methodology involving multichannel saliency estimation and M-SVM for skin lesion segmentation and recognition. The paper details specific experimental procedures, implementation using MATLAB, and quantitative results (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, etc.) on benchmark datasets (PH2, ISBI 2016, ISIC), which are characteristic of original experimental research rather than a review.

title: Skin lesion segmentation and recognition using multichannel saliency estimation and M-SVM on selected serially fused features
journal: Journal of Ambient Intelligence and Humanized Computing
brief_summary: This study addresses the challenge of melanoma detection by proposing a computer-aided diagnosis system for skin lesion segmentation and recognition. The methodology employs multichannel saliency estimation using ternary color spaces for segmentation and a Multiclass Support Vector Machine (M-SVM) for classification after serial feature fusion. The proposed approach was evaluated on three benchmark datasets: PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISIC (including UDA and MSK subsets). Performance was assessed using metrics such as accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and false positive rate via 10-fold cross-validation. The results demonstrated that the M-SVM classifier achieved superior performance, with accuracy rates of 99.2% on ISBI 2016, 97.5% on PH2, and up to 99.5% on ISIC datasets. The authors conclude that their method outperforms existing techniques with a considerable margin in both segmentation accuracy and classification efficiency.

lesion_types_studied.details: Primary malignant class studied across PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISIC datasets. In ISBI 2016, 273 melanoma images were used. In ISIC UDA, 187 melanoma images were used. In ISIC MSK 1&2, 517 melanoma images were used in the confusion matrix.

Primary benign class studied across PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISIC datasets. In ISBI 2016, 1006 benign images were used. In ISIC UDA, 430 benign images were used. In ISIC MSK 1&2, 800 benign images were used in the confusion matrix. The paper notes that benign lesions include common nevi.
Mentioned in the context of clinical methods for assessing presurgical borders (Dhane et al. 2016, 2017; Gambichler et al. 2011), but not included as a target class in the experiments.

all_task_types.details: The paper uses a Multiclass Support Vector Machine (M-SVM) to classify skin lesions into discrete classes: benign (common nevi) and malignant (melanoma). The task is explicitly described as 'recognition' and 'classification' of skin lesions. Performance is evaluated using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, and confusion matrices for these discrete classes.

The paper also performs lesion segmentation using multichannel saliency estimation to separate the foreground (lesion) from the background. This is a secondary task to support the primary classification task.

datasets_used.name: PH2 Dataset
datasets_used.description: A public dermoscopic image dataset used for skin lesion segmentation and classification. It contains RGB images with ground truth segmentations.

use.training: Used for training and testing via 10-fold cross-validation (split not explicitly quantified per fold, but total 200 images used).

datasets_used.name: ISBI 2016 dataset
datasets_used.description: The ISBI 2016 Skin Lesion Analysis Towards Melanoma Detection challenge dataset, consisting of dermoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training with a 50:50 split (639 images).
use.testing: Used for testing with a 50:50 split (640 images).

datasets_used.name: ISIC UDA dataset
datasets_used.description: A subset of the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, specifically the UDA (University of Delft) collection, containing dermoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training with a 50:50 split (308 images).
use.testing: Used for testing with a 50:50 split (309 images).

datasets_used.name: ISIC MSK 1&2 dataset
datasets_used.description: A combination of the MSK-1 and MSK-2 subsets from the International Skin Imaging Collaboration (ISIC) dataset, containing dermoscopic images.

use.training: Used for training with a 50:50 split (1317 images).
use.testing: Used for testing with a 50:50 split (1318 images).

statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to evaluate the performance of the proposed segmentation and classification methods on the PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISIC datasets.
Used within the Neighborhood Component Analysis (NCA) algorithm for feature selection to optimize the expected error of a stochastic KNN classifier.

statistical_methods_used.method: Statistical feature calculation (Mean, Variance, Skewness, Kurtosis)
statistical_methods_used.purpose: Used to extract statistical properties from color channels for feature representation.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance comparisons were made using accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, and other metrics, with results described qualitatively as 'outperforming' existing techniques.

segmentation.notes: Based on top 25 results in Table 1.

classification.notes: M-SVM achieved the best accuracy. ESDA and LDA achieved second and third best accuracy respectively. Confusion matrix shows 40 benign, 40 common nevi, and 20 melanoma testing images.

segmentation.notes: Based on top 25 results in Table 1. Maximum accuracy for selected images was 95.32%.

classification.notes: M-SVM achieved the best accuracy. Confusion matrix shows 503 benign and 136 melanoma testing images.

segmentation.notes: Based on top 25 results in Table 1.

classification.notes: M-SVM achieved the best accuracy. Confusion matrix shows 308 benign and 58 melanoma testing images.

segmentation.notes: Textual result in Section 4.6 states average segmentation accuracy on ISIC MSK1 & MSK 2 is 99%.

classification.notes: M-SVM achieved the best accuracy. Confusion matrix shows 800 benign and 517 melanoma testing images.

architecture_used.main_model: Multichannel Saliency Estimation for segmentation and Multiclass Support Vector Machine (M-SVM) for classification
architecture_used.input_size: Images resized to 256 × 256 for ISIC dataset evaluation; original dimensions varied (e.g., 1022 × 1022, 1504 × 1504 for ISIC UDA)

architecture_details.segmentation: Multichannel saliency estimation using ternary color spaces and multilevel contrast stretching
architecture_details.feature_extraction: Serial fusion of 7 features: asymmetry (4), border (8), diameter (1), shape/HOG (3780), and color (12), resulting in a 1 × 3805 vector
architecture_details.feature_selection: Neighborhood Component Analysis (NCA) for dimensionality reduction
architecture_details.implementation: MATLAB R2017b, built-in image category classifier, GPU (Sapphire R9-290 X Tri-X OC Edition)

training_details.training_schedule: 10-fold cross-validation for PH2, ISBI 2016, and ISIC datasets; 50:50 train/test split for ISBI 2016, ISIC UDA, and ISIC MSK 1&2

preprocessing_techniques: Hair removal using Dull Razor software (algorithmic approach to identify and interpolate hair segments)
Color space transformation to LAB (separation of luminance and chrominance channels)
Contrast stretching (normalization) on selected luminance and other color channels
Sobel edge filtering (3x3 kernel) prior to gradient computation
Image blocking/splitting into equal-sized blocks for weighted region analysis
Morphological operations (closing, filling, and reconstruction) to enhance foreground/background differentiation
conclusions: The proposed method, utilizing multichannel saliency estimation for segmentation and Multiclass Support Vector Machine (M-SVM) for classification, outperforms numerous existing techniques with a considerable margin in both segmentation accuracy and classification efficiency.
The M-SVM classifier achieved superior performance across benchmark datasets, with accuracy rates of 99.2% on ISBI 2016, 97.5% on PH2, 99.5% on ISIC UDA, and 99.0% on ISIC MSK 1&2.
The segmentation method achieved average accuracies of 92.54% on the PH2 dataset, 91.53% on the ISBI 2016 dataset, and 93.28% on the ISIC dataset.

The study demonstrates that the combination of ternary color spaces for foreground-background separation and serial feature fusion (including ABCDE, shape, and color features) effectively supports the detection and recognition of melanoma versus benign skin lesions.

The proposed system serves as an effective computer-aided diagnosis tool to support dermatologists in the initial judgment and early detection of skin lesions, addressing the challenge of distinguishing between benign and malignant tumors which exhibit similar features at early stages.
important_remarks: The study relies on traditional machine learning (M-SVM) and hand-crafted features rather than deep learning, which the authors acknowledge as a limitation and suggest for future work.

The PH2 dataset is small (200 images) and includes Basal Cell Carcinoma (BCC) in the training/testing splits, but the classification results reported are primarily for Melanoma vs. Benign, potentially masking performance on the BCC class.
The ISBI 2016 dataset evaluation uses a 50:50 train-test split, which may not reflect real-world class imbalance or generalization compared to the standard challenge protocol.
The ISIC UDA and MSK datasets were resized to 256x256 pixels for evaluation, which may result in loss of fine-grained diagnostic details present in the original high-resolution dermoscopic images.
No external validation was performed; all models were trained and tested on public benchmark datasets (PH2, ISBI 2016, ISIC subsets), limiting evidence of clinical robustness.

The paper does not report statistical significance tests (e.g., p-values) for the comparison between the proposed method and baseline classifiers, relying instead on point estimates of accuracy and sensitivity.
The implementation uses a specific GPU (Sapphire R9-290 X) and MATLAB R2017b, which may affect reproducibility and execution time comparisons with modern hardware or software environments.

The feature extraction pipeline is complex, involving serial fusion of asymmetry, border, diameter, shape (HOG), and color features, which may limit interpretability and clinical adoption compared to end-to-end deep learning models.
